entry.title: 管理自动付款应用中的附件和注释新功能支持
entry.content:
1. 业务背景

在客户付款流程中，需要根据供应商发票、合同、银行确认或审批备忘录等支持文档来证明和验证付款请求的合理性和真实性。

 如果您正在使用已激活的范围项目 J78 中的工作流功能。目前，管理自动付款应用不支持在付款请求级别上载或处理附件或输入注释的功能。

这会导致：

  • 需要额外的系统外的手动流程（比如通过电子邮件、共享文件夹来分享以上提到的证明文件）
  • 系统外流程导致丢失严密的审计线索链接
  • 审批处理效率低下，需要同时比对系统外原始文档和系统内待审批条目
  • 缺少注释，无法给不知情的审批人解释付款审批的缘由，增加沟通成本
  • 存在合规性和审计风险

云系统时代，客户需要端到端数字付款审批流程，包括提供上载附件、增加注释的功能。

这正是新 SAP CIP “Attachment and Note Support in Manage Automatic Payments”

（在 https://influence.sap.com/sap/ino/#/idea/370881/?section=sectionDetails）至关重要。

  1. 当前限制
  • 通过“管理自动付款”创建的付款请求不允许上载附件或增加注释
  • 工作流审批人无法访问支持文档，需要通过系统外辅助方式来实现
  • 附件不得不在 SAP 外部发送（电子邮件、SharePoint 等）
  • 没有链接到付款凭证的集中审计线索
  1. 请求的增强

要求 1：付款请求中的附件上载

允许用户直接在 管理自动付款 应用中上载附件和增加注释。

详细信息：

  • 每个付款申请允许多个附件
  • 支持常见文件类型（PDF、DOCX、XLSX、JPG、PNG等）
  • 使用 SAP 附件服务安全存储的文件（例如GOS/DMS/附件服务）
  • 附件和注释可见于：
    • 付款请求抬头
    • 项目级别（可选）

业务价值：

  • 完全数字付款请求流程
  • 降低运营间接费用
  • 提高审计就绪程度
  • 提高财务和应付账款团队的透明度

要求 2：通过工作流审批的附件流

确保所有上载的附件在审批工作流中自动可用。

详细信息：

  • 附件应为：
    • 已传递到管理付款审批工作流
    • 对 中的所有审批人可见：
      • 我的收件箱
      • 工作流审批屏幕
  • 附件必须为：
    • 可查看
    • 可下载
  • 基于权限控制访问

业务价值：

  • 更快速、更明智地审批
  • 由于缺少信息，减少审批拒绝
  • 加强内部控制与合规性
  • 从请求到审批再到付款，实现全面的可追溯性
  1. 合规与审计优势
  • 链接到付款交易的集中凭证资源库
  • 为内部和外部审计提供清晰的审计跟踪
  • 与 SOX 和内部财务控制要求保持一致
  • 降低运营风险，减少人为错误
  1. 目标成果

通过实施附件支持和工作流传播：

  • 付款审批完全数字化且合规
  • 审批周期时间显著缩短
  • 客户避免自定义开发或外部工具
  • 功能与 SAP S/4HANA Cloud 最佳实践保持一致
  1. 优先级和影响
  • 优先级：高
  • 影响领域：财务运营、应付账款、内部控制
  • 客户类型：使用自动付款的所有 S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 客户，尤其是审批人一般是公司中高级管理人员，包括但不限于采购部经理，财务部经理，资金部经理，财务总监，运营总监，甚至是总裁等。他们都是对公司运营，包括购买云ERP有决策影响的人，让他们有好的业务体验，非常重要。

✅ 此增强将显著提高管理自动付款应用的可用性、控制和企业准备情况。

entry.title: Re: How can I master the digital business transformation to achieve high and sustainable user adopti
entry.content:
I appreciate Margit sharing this learning journey — the people side of SAP Cloud projects is genuinely critical and often underestimated.

That said, I strongly agree with the spirit of many comments here: true digital business transformation isn't mastered through a tool or a single learning journey. It's a continuous, organization-wide discipline.

From my experience as VP of IT leading multiple transformations, sustainable user adoption only happens when:

  • The solution solves real business pain users feel daily
  • Leadership treats adoption as a 12–24 month journey, not a go-live event
  • We remove friction instead of just pushing training & communication
  • We measure business outcomes, not system usage metrics

The SAP OCM learning path is helpful for project-level execution, but real mastery comes from combining strong change management with ruthless process simplification and cultural alignment.

Curious to hear from others — what's the biggest adoption blocker you've faced in your SAP/cloud transformations?

Hi @MelissaME ,

I saw your conversation with Margit about the Managing the People Side of SAP Cloud Projects learning journey and your question regarding training management for both new S/4HANA implementations and upgrades.

I work with SAP customers on organizational change management and training enablement. Rather than jumping in with offers, I'm genuinely curious:

What exactly are you trying to accomplish with this certification? And what pain points are you currently facing — or intend to solve — when it comes to managing training phases and user adoption across new implementations vs. upgrades?

Are you struggling with:

  • Getting business users to actually adopt the new system post-go-live?

  • Aligning your training approach across different SAP Activate phases?

  • Bridging the gap between Change Management and Training Management on projects?

  • Something else entirely?

Would love to understand your specific situation before saying anything useful.

Best regards,

Venkat

entry.title: Re: Consumption Mode 5 with Planning Period P
entry.content: Pure configuration is not possible for fiscal period PIR consumption in SAP. Consumption mode 5 is limited to M/W periods. Custom BAdI implementation is required to handle fiscal periods and flexible backward/forward offsets.

entry.title: Re: Does pMRP Consider Routing Operation Overlapping and...
entry.content:
Please note pMRP is designed as a strategic simulation tool to evaluate capacity and material availability under different demand scenarios. It deliberately simplifies the lead‑time calculation model compared to classic MRP. As a result, detailed scheduling elements such as operation overlapping maintained in routings and lead‑time offsets maintained in BOM components are not considered in pMRP runs. Instead, pMRP uses basic lead‑time data (procurement type, in‑house production time, planned delivery time, etc.) to simulate material flows and capacity requirements.

If you need simulations that respect detailed scheduling logic (like overlaps and offsets), you would need to rely on classic MRP or detailed scheduling in PP/DS rather than pMRP.

entry.title: SAP Business Data Cloud and Datasphere News in April
entry.content:
SAP Business Data Cloud and Datasphere News in April

SAPPHIRE season is upon us 🌴 and the April releases didn't hold back either — plenty of useful features landing across monitoring, task chains, data products, and more.

Learn more in my top features video in April 2026 on YouTube 📺.

In addition, explore the latest updates in our community news blogs and more.
 

 

My top features in April

Update Action for Installed Data Products

Data products installed in an SAP Datasphere space now have an Update action button, available when new minor versions are released. This improvement streamlines the workflow, providing a more efficient and intuitive process for users tasked with keeping data products current. 
➡️  More information Installing Data Products and Data Product Details.

 

BDC Cockpit Data Product Monitoring

As promised, the BDC Cockpit is being continuously improved. Administrators now get significantly better visibility into SAP-managed data product pipelines — this is the first step of several improvements coming over the next months.

The cockpit now shows the full pipeline status at every step, making it easy to identify exactly where issues occur and act before they impact your business.

 

Local Table Partitioning

In the table editor, you can create partitions on existing SAP-Managed local tables stored in a standard space (with SAP HANA Database (Disk and In-Memory) storage) that already contain data. So previously unpartitioned tables can now be partitioned even if they already carry data. This is particularly useful for large-volume tables where partitioning was not possible at initial creation. 
➡️  More information Partitioning Local Tables and Partitioning Local Tables for Intelligent Applications.

 

Spark Configuration

The Limits to the amount of compute resources that Apache Spark applications can consume when running tasks are set with configurations. In addition to using the provided configurations, which are applied by default to new file spaces, you can now change the default configuration for a task activity in each file space, and create your own configurations.

Be aware, that newly provided default configurations for task activities may allocate fewer resources than those used in previous versions (see SAP Note 3726059), tasks might run more slowly even though they consume less compute. If these new default values don't meet your needs, you can change the default configuration for a task activity in each file space. 
➡️  More information.

 

Cloud Connector Support for Google BigQuery

You can now configure private connectivity for Google BigQuery connections through the Cloud Connector. The Cloud Connector routes data traffic through secure tunnels rather than the public internet, allowing you to use replication flows to write data to Google BigQuery target systems that don't have public endpoints. 
➡️  More information.

 

Task Chains Deletion Types

Task chains now support all deletion types for both local tables and Local Tables (file).

The newly supported deletion activities are:

  • Delete All Records
  • Delete Filtered Records

➡️  More information.

 

Semantic Onboarding of Calc Views from HANA Cloud

You can import calculation views from SAP HANA Cloud as remote tables while preserving semantic information. This helps you leverage existing HANA Cloud models without recreating them, keeping their business context and metadata for consistent data modeling across platforms.

Be aware that as a prerequisite, only calculation views deployed in SAP HANA Cloud version QRC 1/2026 or higher can be imported in SAP Datasphere database version 2025.50 or higher.

To ensure that your calculation view can be imported, deploy the calculation view in the source system with SAP Business Data Cloud Integration Mode set to Flat View or Construction Kit.

➡️  More information

  • Importing Entities with Semantics from SAP HANA Cloud
  • SAP HANA Connections
 

Analytic Model — Inherited and Local Properties

A small but useful addition to the analytic model. For analytic models that use another analytic model as source, you can switch on Show Inherited Elements in the properties panel, to display the inherited elements. That makes it easy to distinguish between local and inherited properties. 
➡️  More information.

 

Improved Monitoring Tool Navigation

The Monitoring tool now features an enhanced navigation menu for easier access to key monitoring pages:

  • System and Spaces – Monitor system performance, storage, tasks, and out-of-memory issues across all spaces. Track storage consumption and Apache Spark usage.
  • Capacities Monitoring – Track monthly and daily capacity unit consumption against your subscription and download detailed hourly data.
  • Task Logs – Review task execution history and performance.
  • Expensive Statement Logs – Monitor SQL statements exceeding configured thresholds.
  • Elastic Compute Nodes - Monitor elastic compute nodes key performance figures.
  • Data Integration – Monitor, schedule, and run data replication tasks for remote tables and views. Manage flows and task chains.

That consolidates all the monitoring activities into one menu section. 
➡️  More information.

 

 

SAPPHIRE 2026 — Let's Meet in Orlando

SAP SAPPHIRE 2026 is just around the corner! I'll be in Orlando presenting the introductory session on SAP Business Data Cloud — a 20-minute guided tour of what it is, how it's positioned, and where it fits into SAP's data and analytics strategy. I'm running it twice:

📅 Monday, May 11 | 1:30 PM – 1:50 PM EDT

📅 Tuesday, May 12 | 4:00 PM – 4:20 PM EDT

With 30+ sessions on Business Data Cloud at this year's SAPPHIRE, there's a lot to explore. You'll also find us at the SAP Business Data Cloud booth with six demo stations and hands-on sessions. No excuse not to stop by if you're in Orlando.

Can't make it in person? All content will be available online — and of course, I'll keep publishing the monthly newsletter right here. 
🔗  Session link  |  🔗  My SAPPHIRE blog

 

Webinar Series: The Next Era of Business Data

The last session of the great series has just been streamed on YouTube. In case you could not make it live, all sessions are recorded and I added the links to the recordings below.

🔹Jan 29 | Discover SAP Business Data Cloud in 2026 - recording

🔹Feb 05 | The Smart Path to SAP BW Modernization - recording

🔹Feb 19 | Empowering SAP Datasphere Users with SAP Business Data Cloud Innovations - recording

🔹Mar 26 | Design. Govern. Deliver. Data Products - Data Product Studio - recording

👉  NEW 🔹 Apr 16 | Breaking Down the SAP BDC Pricing Model - recording

entry.title: Re: BAPI/FM/API for KKPAN in SAP S4HANA
entry.content:
To my knowledge, there is no standard BAPI or released API for transaction KKPAN. SAP has not provided a direct function module for mass unit costing; the usual approach is either batch input (BDC), using report programs, or leveraging enhancement frameworks.

Having said that, the recommended path can be

1) For mass unit costing
Build a custom ABAP report that either uses BDC for KKPAN or wraps internal costing FMs. Ensure logging and error handling for materials that fail costing.

2) For long-term stability
Raise a request with SAP if you need a released API. SAP may suggest alternatives like using product cost planning APIs (which exist for CK transactions, but not for KK).

entry.title: Re: Provide a CDS view for Overhead rate in Costing Sheet
entry.content: The CDS view you need is I_OverheadRateInCostingSheet, already released in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud. You can build your own analytical query on top of it to provide the month‑end overhead rate report for your customer.

entry.title: Re: Stock transfer price differs in supplying and receivi...
entry.content:
SAP standard does not allow you to simply “change the valuation price” during stock transfer, but there are supported ways to model this

Some of the standard options are

1. Stock Transport Order (STO) with Condition Technique
Create an intercompany STO between supplying plant M121 and the receiving plant. In the STO pricing procedure, maintain a pricing condition (e.g., PB00) at 700 INR (market price). The supplying plant issues delivery and billing; the receiving plant posts GR at 700 INR. This way, the receiving plant inventory is valued at 700 INR, while the supplying plant still carries 1000 INR cost in its books. The difference (300 INR) is posted to intercompany clearing accounts.

2. Transfer Pricing in Controlling
SAP supports multiple valuation approaches (legal, group, profit center). You can configure transfer prices so that Supplying plant uses cost-based valuation (1000 INR). Receiving plant uses market-based valuation (700 INR). This requires activation of parallel valuation in CO and customizing transfer pricing.

3. Special Procurement Key
Define a special procurement key for the receiving plant to source from M121. Combine with STO pricing so that the valuation in the receiving plant reflects the market price.

entry.title: Guidance on calling S4 OData API from SAP IS using OAUTH.
entry.content:
Hi,

We were looking into options to see how to make the OData calls to S4 API from SAP IS using OAUTH Authentication. Curretly it is working fine with Basic Authentication.

We tried to follow the below blog posts.

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/step-by-step-calling-s-4hana-private-cloud-standard-odata-apis-with-oauth-2/ba-p/14346113

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/sap-integration-suite-connecting-to-s-4hana-private-cloud-odata-with-oauth/ba-p/14295261

 

We have couple of questions which we are not very clear.

 

  1.  It seems like a 2 step process to make the OData calls. One Http call to get the token and another ODAta call by passing the bearer token. There is no OAUTH2 Authentication availabe for OData connector when trying to call the SAP Private Cloud API. So, to get the token we need to pass the username/password as the client_id / secrets. This means we need to store the username/password in Secutrity material and pass that for the http call. Will this make OAUTH Authentication more secure than Basic Authentication ?
  2. If we use the OAUTH approach, how is the access to the API works ? The OAuth token will only have access to the API (Scope) configured on the OAUTH client  ? Do we need to grant any roles to the SU01 user to get access to the API explicitly ?  If yes, then the Basic Authentication also works ?

 

It might be easy if we can have a call to clarify these.

 

Cheers

Anoop

entry.title: Why Knowledge Transition Looks Complete but Still Fails After S/4HANA Go-Live
entry.content:
Why Knowledge Transition Looked Complete but Still Failed After Our S/4HANA Go-Live

Introduction

Over the years, I’ve been involved in many SAP AMS transitions, covering everything from S/4HANA and ECC to Basis operations and production support handovers. One thing I’ve noticed time and again is that knowledge transition (KT) often ends up feeling like just another box to tick.

Sessions get done, documents get uploaded, trackers turn green, and everyone feels ready to move into support. But in one of my S/4HANA projects, I learned the hard way that ticking all the KT boxes on paper doesn’t always mean the AMS team is truly prepared for the realities of production.

This experience changed how I now approach KT, especially for complex Finance environments with lots of interfaces.

The Project Situation

This project involved:

  • SAP S/4HANA implementation
  • FI integration with external accounting systems
  • Multiple inbound interfaces using IDocs
  • Batch job-heavy month-end processing
  • AMS takeover after hypercare

I was coordinating between the project team, Basis team, and AMS support teams to ensure support readiness. During KT, everything seemed very organised. The project team gave detailed walkthroughs covering:

  • FI posting flows
  • IDoc architecture
  • Background job schedules
  • Monitoring activities
  • Common error handling steps
  • Escalation contacts

The Basis team also explained standard monitoring transactions and daily health checks.

By the end of KT:

  • All KT trackers were marked complete
  • Sign-offs were obtained
  • Shadow support was completed
  • Readiness status was green

At that point, everyone felt confident.

What Happened After Go-Live

 The system was stable for the first few weeks after go-live. But the real challenge hit during the first month-end closing cycle.

I still remember one late-night support call when the finance team reported delays in accounting postings coming from external systems. At first, nothing seemed critical:

  • No dumps in ST22
  • No major system alerts
  • Background jobs were running
  • Interfaces were technically active

The AMS team’s first thought was, “The system looks fine. Let’s check with the project team.” But as we dug deeper, we noticed unusual queue behaviour:

  • During one month-end cycle, we noticed nearly 8,000 inbound FI IDocs sitting in status 64 for more than 20 minutes.


     

    Example of inbound FI IDocs waiting in status 64 during peak month-end processing.


  • SM58 showed growing tRFC entries

Example of queue buildup observed during high-volume month-end processing.

  • SMQ1 queues gradually building up
  • Some FI-related background jobs that normally finished within minutes were taking significantly longer during peak load.

The tricky part was there was no outright system failure. The issue crept in slowly under peak load. While the AMS team knew the monitoring tools and technical setup, they weren’t fully confident whether this was just normal temporary load or an early warning of a bottleneck. That uncertainty delayed troubleshooting and escalation decisions.

What We Realised Was Missing in KT

After things stabilised, we reviewed the KT approach and realised something important:

The KT sessions had focused heavily on system design and support steps, but not enough on how the system actually behaves in real production.

We covered:

  • Configuration walkthroughs
  • Interface structures
  • Monitoring steps
  • Job schedules
  • Known issue handling

But we hadn’t talked enough about:

  • System behaviour during peak month-end load
  • Queue buildup patterns seen in performance testing
  • Critical timing overlaps between interfaces and batch jobs
  • Temporary manual monitoring done during hypercare
  • Known “sensitive windows” during closing cycles

I remember a project team member saying, “We saw similar queue buildup during testing, but it cleared automatically, so we didn’t think it was a big risk.”

That stuck with me. If we had openly shared that during KT, the AMS team could have reacted much faster in production.

What We Changed in Later Projects

After that, we changed how we handle KT in future S/4HANA projects. Instead of just transferring system knowledge, we started focusing on operational realities.

During KT, we began asking questions like:

  • Where did the system struggle during testing?
  • Which interfaces are most sensitive during peak load?
  • Which batch jobs often overlap?
  • What temporary monitoring was needed during hypercare?
  • What warning signs should AMS teams watch for?

From the Basis side, these discussions became especially valuable. We started documenting:

  • Expected queue growth patterns
  • Critical SM58 monitoring scenarios
  • High-risk job execution windows
  • Typical month-end workload spikes
  • Situations where manual intervention was needed before

We also improved shadow support. Instead of immediately passing issues back to the project team, we encouraged AMS teams to try initial troubleshooting themselves, with project teams available in the background. It slowed things down at first but built much stronger support confidence after go-live.

Key Learning

The biggest lesson I learned is that knowledge transfer isn’t just about explaining the system. It’s about sharing real operational experience.

Documents can cover configurations, transactions, and monitoring steps, but true production readiness comes from understanding how the system behaves under pressure, during busy periods, and when things don’t go as planned.

In most projects, that knowledge stays with the project team unless someone makes a conscious effort to share it during KT.

Conclusion

In many SAP projects, KT is treated as a milestone to tick off before AMS takeover. But from what I’ve seen, successful KT depends less on how many documents are shared and more on honest conversations about real operational behaviour.

Sometimes the most valuable KT discussions aren’t about system architecture at all. They’re about questions like:

  • Where did we struggle during testing?
  • What nearly broke during hypercare?
  • Which processes make the team nervous at month-end?

Those conversations prepare AMS teams far better than any checklist can.

In my experience, KT truly succeeds only when teams share not just system knowledge, but also the operational judgement built through the project journey.

entry.title: Re: The Requested By field in My Purchase Requisition app...
entry.content:
Hello @Subodh_Chordiya,

the field „Requested by“ from App My Purchase Requisitions does not exist in App Manage Purchase Requisitions – Professional.

Instead, Manage Purchase Requisitions – Professional contains the field “Requisitioner”.

 

My Purchase Requisitions:

Manage Purchase Requisitions – Professional:

 

 

The field “Requisitioner” may be used for the same purpose, however there are two caveats:

  • It does not contain a list of all employees. Instead, it is a text input field.
  • It is not automatically a mandatory field. By using custom logic as described in blog post Setting Mandatory Fields in Purchase Requisitions ... - SAP Community it can be set as mandatory.

 

„Requested by“ from App My Purchase Requisitions can not be made available in App Manage Purchase Requisitions – Professional, as „Requested by“ is specific for Self-Service Purchasing.

 

Best regards,

Kerisha

entry.title: Re: EC Time Off- Update custom field in Job Information w...
entry.content:
Hi @RishabhGupta19881 

Your requirement on updating a custom field in job information based on specific time type Approval, it should be achievable through integration centre by creating a job with source as Employee Time object and destination as Jobinfo object with advanced filter for Time type and it's approval status. 

You have to schedule this job based on the business requirement either to auto-schedule one time per day or multiple intervals in a same day. 

Kindly refer below KBA blog to achieve your requirement, the steps to create the job was shared by me to one of the query had from expert. 

Solved: Automatic update of custom MDF object data - SAP Community

entry.title: 为什么中高层管理人员的支付审批体验在 ERP 云中至关重要
entry.content:
1.背景概要

对于使用SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 中的 银企直联(EPIC)付款审批工作流的用户，相关的中高层管理人员 (CxO)（付款审批链中的最终决策者）在审核和审批付款申请时的用户体验至关重要。

中层管理人员（采购部经理，财务部经理，资金部经理等，根据审批金额大小按照流程进行设定），进一步甚至是CxO级别，他们必须跨多个标签和屏幕导航以从财务凭证下钻到原始物流凭证，例如：采购订单、采购订单收货过账凭证等，并收集做出明智的批准或拒绝决策所需的信息。这对他们不是直接在系统进行操作级别的任务来说，现有云ERP系统缺少必要的，直接的，便捷的数据信息提供给利益相关方，这样就带来了潜在的审批风险、导致审批延迟和进一步不满意系统导致抵制使用云ERP系统的动力，也就是说审批页面的使用的友好性影响利益相关方对 ERP 云部署的认可，而这些审批人对公司决策富有极大的影响力，包括云ERP的继续订阅至关重要。

这正是新的CIP“我的付款申请收件箱中的 CxO 付款审批用户体验 - 中国”

（在  https://influence.sap.com/sap/ino/#/idea/372233/?section=sectionDetails）如此重要

2.客户背景

 

客户

中国的云ERP客户

解决方案

使用中国本地化的银企直联方案

模块

财务/应付账款 - 付款申请审批

工作流

多级付款审批：级别 1（附加并提交）→ 级别 2 → 级别 3 → 级别 4 → 级别 5 → 级别 6（如果可能）

审批人

中高层管理人员（采购部经理，会计经理、财务部经理，首席财务官、首席执行官、首席运营官、公司拥有者等）—最终决策者

3.现状 - 痛点

当前“审批付款申请”屏幕（标准 S/4HANA Cloud）（应用：我的付款申请收件箱 - 中国，应用标识：F5444）在六个单独的标签中显示信息：常规信息、请求的项目、付款策略、附件、审批详细信息和管理数据。

  • 分散信息 所有付款详细信息均分布在 6 个标签中。在制定决策之前，中高级管理人员必须点击每个选项卡，形成完整的视图。没有合并汇总视图。中高级管理人员始终使用移动、键盘来处理工作流活动，屏幕设计对他们来说非常重要，他们可以使用所提供的信息。
  • 无附件可见性 概览“附件”部分（原始业务文件 - 发票、合同、支持文档）位于单独的选项卡上。中高级管理人员无法查看是否在不离开主要信息的情况下附加文档。
  • 审批和业务数据未并置工作流步骤 (1.附加并提交 → 2.级别 2 → 3。级别 3 → 4 → 5 → 6。完成）显示在审批详细信息中，而财务数据在申请的项目中。CxO 必须在标签页之间翻转，根据实际交易进行交叉引用审批。
  • 缺少的关键字段关键字段（例如采购订单参考、物料、单位成本、总金额和毛利）不会显示在标准视图中，从而使 CxO 没有自信审批所需的业务上下文。
  • 批准/拒绝操作不可见 无需滚动或导航，主屏幕上不会立即显示“批准”和“拒绝”按钮，这会给时间受限的高管带来摩擦。
  • 移动不友好的 CxO 经常移动化。当前多标签布局未针对移动审核进行优化，导致主管无法随时随地审批。

4.未来设计 - 建议的增强

建议的“审批付款应用程序”屏幕将所有信息合并到单个可滚动接口。设计目标包括：信息一目了然、移动优先布局和一键式决策。

  • 单页合并视图所有部分 - 基本信息、应用程序项目、付款策略、附件、审批详细信息和管理数据 - 显示在一个可滚动页面上。不需要标签导航。
  • 直接显示添加到应用程序项目的新字段关键业务字段：采购订单编号、物料、单价、总金额（包括税款）、毛利和行级成本详细信息。中高级管理人员立即全面了解财务状况。
  • 内联附件上载和审核 使用“添加附件”按钮在同一页面上内联显示附件部分。无需切换标签即可立即访问原始业务文件（发票、合同）。
  • 审批工作流在完整上下文中可见 所有工作流步骤（1.附加并提交，2.级别 2、3。级别 3、4。完成）显示在审批详细信息部分，其中包含处理人姓名、状态和时间戳 - 与财务数据在同一页面上。
  • 审批和拒绝按钮始终可见“批准”和“拒绝”操作按钮锚定在界面底部，始终显示为 CxO 滚动，确保一键式决策无需寻找按钮。
  • 移动-自适应布局 单页设计适应移动屏幕，使中高级管理人员能够随时通过任何设备查看所有相关信息，并制定审批决策。
  • 打开行以更新记录内联编辑功能允许授权用户直接在审批屏幕中更新记录，而无需导航到单独的事务

5.现状与未来差距分析

 

维度

现状

目标

影响

信息布局

多选项卡（6 个选项卡）

单个可滚动页

HIGH

原始凭证

在附件标签中隐藏

内嵌，始终可见

HIGH

财务详细信息字段

仅基本字段

完整字段，包括采购订单、物料、利润

HIGH

审批步骤可见性

单独审批标签

与财务数据互联

中等

操作按钮

需要滚动/导航

锚定，始终可见

HIGH

移动可用性

较差 - 移动设备上的标签导航中断

优化的单页滚动

HIGH

直接编辑

不可用

用于更新记录的未清行

中等

6.为什么中高级管理人员的体验对 ERP 云的成功至关重要

中高级管理人员不是普通的 ERP 用户，他们是高管发起人，他们对该系统的认同、满意度和日常体验直接决定了 ERP 云转型的成就。以下几点强调了必须划分用户体验优先级的原因：

  • 决策机构 CxO 持有付款交易的最终审批权力。工作流中的任何不顺畅，不便利都会直接转化为公司付款周期的瓶颈，从而影响供应商关系和现金流。
  • 采用风险 如果中高级管理人员的体验不佳，高管将拒绝或规避系统。这是任何 ERP 云推广中的单一最大采用风险，如果领导层不使用系统，则会向整个组织发出负面信号。
  • 样板客户 价值 客户满意的中高级管理人员成为 SAP 云解决方案的样板客户。反之，令人沮丧的 CxO 成为客户内进一步扩展 SAP 和市场负面参考的抑制因素。
  • 合规与治理 中高级管理人员通常根据中国公司治理法规依法批准付款。审批屏幕必须提供足够的信息，以便主管履行信托和监管职责。
  • 战略信号引导市场在 ERP 云中应优先考虑中高级管理人员用户体验，这表明 SAP 了解企业决策动态。这将 SAP 定位为客户数字化转型之旅的合作伙伴，而不仅仅是软件供应商。
  • 时间价值 CxOs 的时间是任何组织中最昂贵的资源。每次切换标签时，每次屏幕导航都会浪费时间。简化的审批体验是尊重高管时间的直接声明。

7.结论

建议对付款审批工作流的用户体验增强不是外观改进 - 这是一项战略必要条件。中高级管理人员是企业 ERP 部署中最具影响力的用户。他们能够查看完整的业务信息，并自信地制定一键式审批决策，这对于 SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 的运营效率和采用成功至关重要。

当前的多选项卡现状的设计迫使管理人员浏览分散的信息，增加认知负荷，引入批准延误，并冒着行政人员拒绝该系统的风险。计划设计的单页整合设计直接解决所有确定的痛点，并与基于角色、直观和移动优先用户体验的 SAP Fiori 设计理念保持一致。

中高级管理人员不仅仅是最终用户，而是 ERP 云转型的拥护者。中高级管理人员的构建意味着为整个实施的成功而构建。

entry.title: UI5 MCP Servers Setup Guide + Real Developer Experience
entry.content:
Introduction 

As a developer working on a hybrid UI5 project, I explored and configured UI5 MCP (Model Context Protocol) servers to improve my workflow. What started as a setup exercise quickly turned into a major productivity upgrade. 

This guide combines a step-by-step setup tutorial with real-world experience, so you not only know how to set it up, but also why it actually matters. 

What You’ll Get 

After setting this up, you’ll have: 

  • Instant access to UI5 API documentation  
  • Component discovery without opening a browser  
  • On-demand code examples  
  • Built-in best practices guidance  
  • TypeScript-aware development support  
  • Everything directly inside your IDE  

 

My Project Context 

I worked on a hybrid application: 

  • Traditional UI5 App (SAPUI5)  
  • React App using UI5 Web Components  

This setup made it clear how both MCP servers complement each other. 

Prerequisites 

Before starting, ensure you have: 

  • Visual Studio Code (v1.85 or later)  
  • Node.js (v18 or later)  

Verify installation: 

node --version   
npm --version   

Step 1: Install Cline Extension 

Cline acts as the AI assistant that connects to MCP servers. 

Option A: From VS Code 

  • Open VS Code  
  • Go to Extensions (Ctrl+Shift+X)  
  • Search for Cline  
  • Install the extension by Saoud Rizwan  
  • Restart VS Code if required  

Option B: Command Line 

code --install-extension saoudrizwan.claude-dev 

Verify 

  • Open sidebar  
  • Click Cline icon  
  • You should see a chat interface  

Step 2: Configure MCP Servers 

Open settings.json and add: 

"@ui5/mcp-server": { 

      "autoApprove": [ 

        "get_guidelines", 

        "run_ui5_linter" 

      ], 

      "disabled": false, 

      "timeout": 60, 

      "type": "stdio", 

      "command": "npx", 

      "args": [ 

        "-y", 

        "@ui5/mcp-server" 

      ] 

    }, 

    "ui5-wcr": { 

      "autoApprove": [ 

        "create_app", 

        "get_component_api", 

        "get_documentation", 

        "get_public_utils", 

        "list_components" 

      ], 

      "timeout": 60, 

      "type": "stdio", 

      "command": "npx", 

      "args": [ 

        "@ui5/webcomponents-react-mcp@latest" 

      ] 

    }

 

Save the file. 

Step 3: Restart VS Code 

This step is mandatory. 

  • Close VS Code  
  • Reopen  
  • Open your project  

 

Step 4: Verify Installation 

Try these in Cline: 

  • “List available MCP tools”  
  • “Get UI5 guidelines”  
  • “List UI5 components”  
  • “What are props for Dialog?”  

If everything works, you’re ready. 

My Experience Using MCP Servers 

1. Instant API Access 

Before: constant browser switching 
Now: everything inside IDE 

Example: 
Query any UI5 component and instantly get props, events, and methods. 

2.  Component Discovery 

Finding the right component used to be slow. 

Now I can: 

  • Filter by category  
  • Explore 200+ components  
  • Get import statements instantly  

 3. Built-in Linting 

The UI5 linter helps: 

  • Detect deprecated APIs  
  • Suggest fixes  
  • Maintain clean code  

This was especially useful in traditional UI5 code. 

4. Best Practices on Demand 

Using guidelines: 

  • Avoid common mistakes  
  • Follow proper architecture  
  • Build scalable UI5 apps  

 5. Rapid App Creation 

With MCP tools, I could: 

  • Generate apps with templates  
  • Auto-configure TypeScript  
  • Setup projects instantly  

 

 

Real Workflow Impact 

Before 

  • Write code  
  • Open browser  
  • Search docs  
  • Copy info  
  • Return to IDE  
  • Fix issues manually  

After 

  • Write code  
  • Ask AI  
  • Get instant answers  
  • Auto-fix issues  
  • Continue in flow  

Time saved: ~30–40% 

 

Key Benefits I Noticed 

  • No context switching  
  • Faster debugging  
  • Cleaner code  
  • Better architecture decisions  
  • Offline documentation access  

 

 

Challenges 

  • Initial setup takes effort  
  • Learning tool usage takes time  
  • Understanding when to use which tool  

But once past that, the experience is smooth. 

 

Best Practices 

Do 

  • Use guidelines before building features  
  • Run linter regularly  
  • Use component discovery tools  
  • Query APIs instead of Googling  

Don’t 

  • Ignore linter warnings  
  • Skip best practices  
  • Use wrong tools for the task  

 

Why npx is Used 

  • No global install needed  
  • Always runs latest version  
  • Quick execution  

 

Troubleshooting 

Common issues and fixes: 

  • MCP not working → Restart VS Code  
  • npx not found → Install Node.js  
  • Slow response → First-time download delay  
  • No panel → Open Cline manually  

 

Final Thoughts 

MCP servers fundamentally changed how I work with UI5. 

They: 

  • Reduced friction  
  • Improved code quality  
  • Made development faster and smarter  

For any developer working with UI5 or React-based UI5 components, this setup is absolutely worth it. 

References & Useful Links

  • UI5 Official Documentation
    https://ui5.sap.com/
  • UI5 SDK (API Reference)
    https://ui5.sap.com/#/api
  • UI5 Web Components for React
    https://sap.github.io/ui5-webcomponents-react/
  • UI5 MCP Server (npm)
    https://www.npmjs.com/package/@ui5/mcp-server
  • UI5 Web Components MCP Server
    https://www.npmjs.com/package/@ui5/webcomponents-react-mcp
  • Visual Studio Code
    https://code.visualstudio.com/
  • Node.js
    https://nodejs.org/
  • Cline Extension (VS Code Marketplace)
    https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=saoudrizwan.claude-dev
  • SAP Fiori Design Guidelines
    https://experience.sap.com/fiori-design/
  • OpenUI5 GitHub Repository
    https://github.com/SAP/openui5

entry.title: SAP Signavio announces a new partnership with Tangible Growth
entry.content:
Last week, we announced a strategic partnership with Tangible Growth, a strategy execution platform, to help organizations better connect strategic goals with ongoing business transformation initiatives.

This announcement is particularly exciting for me, as I’ve had the opportunity to work with the Tangible Growth team for the past few years and have seen firsthand the potential of bringing strategy and operations closer together. We at SAP Signavio have also been using the platform internally, which has given us valuable experience in understanding how strategic priorities can be more effectively connected to execution and operational outcomes.

Traditionally, strategic decision-making and business process management have operated in separate worlds. Strategy is often defined at the leadership level, while operational execution happens across processes, systems, and teams with limited real-time alignment between the two.

Together with Tangible Growth and SAP Signavio, we aim to change that status quo. By connecting strategic priorities directly to business transformation initiatives, organizations can create a stronger link between business goals and operational execution. Business Transformation Management provides the foundation across processes, technology, and adoption, eliminating the need to manage the strategic layer manually through disconnected presentations, spreadsheets, and status updates.

More importantly, this integration helps address a common challenge many organizations face today: by the time information reaches decision-makers, it is often already outdated or incomplete. With a tighter connection between strategy and operational reality, leadership teams can identify much earlier when strategic intent is not translating into execution. This enables faster, more informed decision-making and helps organizations continuously steer transformation toward measurable business outcomes.

To deliver real ROI, AI agents need more than just data - they need a clear line of sight to what the business is trying to achieve. By combining SAP Signavio’s AI Agent Excellence with Tangible Growth’s strategic context, we ensure that strategic context acts as the essential guardrail, preventing AI from merely optimizing for local metrics and instead driving measurable business value.

So what does this partnership mean for organizations in practice?

  • Process improvements that directly support strategic objectives
  • Strategic decisions grounded in real-time operational insights
  • Accelerated time to value across transformation initiatives
  • AI-assisted workflows with the strategic context needed to drive better decision-making

As organizations continue to navigate increasingly complex transformations, the ability to connect strategy, operations, and AI-driven execution will become more critical than ever. This partnership is an important step toward making that connection a reality and help organizations turn strategy into measurable business outcomes faster and more effectively.

Check out the video below to hear directly from our teams about the partnership and how we’re helping organizations better connect strategy with operational execution!

Further information

  • Tangible Growth + SAP — Strategic Partnership | Tangible Growth
  • Tangible Growth on SAP Store
  • Press Release

entry.title: PRODUCTION/QC/SALES TRANSACTIONS DONE THROUGH BARCODE SCANNING
entry.content:
Hello Community,

We have the requirement to map in SAP S/4 HANA Cloud Public Edition; I'm sharing the same need guidance how can we execute this in SAP S/4 HANA Public Cloud 

Requirement - 

 

PP / QM Process Understanding:

  • After completion of machining, the finished goods (FG) material should be carefully placed into the designated trolley. Ensure that the material is loaded strictly according to the trolley’s defined capacity to avoid damage, overloading, or handling issues.

 

  • Once the trolley is properly loaded, it should be transferred to the Quality Inspection area in a safe and controlled manner.

 

  • In the Quality area, the Quality Inspector performs a thorough inspection of the products to verify that they meet all required specifications and standards. Only the accepted (OK) quantity is considered for further processing. Based on this approved quantity, a QR code is generated.

 

  • Storage Location is Mandatory

 

  • The generated QR code must accurately capture and display all essential production details, including Production Order Number, Machine Number, Product Name, and Quantity. This ensures complete traceability and data integrity.

 

  • QR code generation is strictly dependent on stock availability in the system. The system should allow QR code generation only after the stock has been officially created and recorded. If the stock is not available in the system, QR code generation must be restricted to prevent discrepancies.

 

  • After successful QR code generation, the Quality Inspector securely affixes the QR code label onto the trolley. The labeled trolley is then moved to the Finished Goods (FG) area, where it is stored or prepared for dispatch.

 

 

 

SD Process Flow

Sales Order (VA01 / Fiori App)
↓
QR Scan App (Custom Fiori)
↓
Outbound Delivery Creation (API / BAPI)
↓
Picking (Auto)
↓
PGI (Post Goods Issue)
↓
Billing Document (Auto)
↓
E-Invoice + E-Way Bill (India Localization)
↓
Digital Signature
↓
Reference Number (Custom Object)

 

  1. Custom Fiori App + Custom Business Object (CBO) For QR Code Scanning
  2. Need to create custom app or Z table to store the below data

Fields:

  • QR Code
  • Material
  • Quantity
  • Plant
  • Storage Location
  • Sales Order
  • Vehicle Number
  • Status
  • Reference Number

QR Code contain - Material + Batch + Qty + Plant

 

The approach for delivery Business Object if available for Delivery creation and PGI for both (Need to check feasibility from Technical end)

 

OR

           Handling Unit (better option if EWM used)

           Create Custom Fiori App for QR scanning

 

Scan QR
Enter:

Customer Code – User will enter manually (Ref Customer Code system will automatically pick active SO)
Sales Order – System must automatically pick the current month’s Sales order based on the Material Code and Customer Code
Customer PO No – System must display automatically once sales order is picked

Vehicle Number – User will enter manually
Validate stock

Invoice No – Display

E-Invoice - Display

E-way Bill No - Display

ASN No – Display

System must generate a single Invoice against multiple QR codes of same Material and Sales Order

Check Box required for each Invoice Line so that user can only select the Invoices who’s ASN is generated. Remaining Invoices must be available for ASN generation and can be added in different Reference Number for this Add Button required

Cancelled Invoices must not appeared in the Pending List of Invoices appearing after clicking Add Button

Invoices for which E-Invoice, E-way bill successfully are allowed to print –Print Button required (Multiple)

Download Button required to download Invoice PDF (Multiple)


Store entries in Z custom table or can create custom CDS to store the data

To create delivery and PGI need to use Business Object or EWM handling unit (Best option)

  1. Use the Outbound Delivery (A2X) API to create an outbound delivery.
  2. Include the necessary information such as the sales order number and item quantity.
  3. The system will perform checks for restricted values and authorizations.
  4. Once the outbound delivery is created, the system will send the delivery number and data.
  5. To post goods issue, utilize the function within the API to reverse goods issue if needed.

To Create Billing Document same approach Business Object / API - BADI (SD_BIL_DATA_TRANSFER)

For E-invoice automation we have already configuration node so using configuration we can achieve this

For Eway bill we have BADI using same we can integrate E-way Bill directly to government portal and achieve this

 

 

For E-way bill automation need to use below details

Name : BADI_EDOCUMENT_IN_EWB
Method: IF_EDOCUMENT_IN_EWB~CHANGE_HEADER_DETAILS
Structure: EDOC_IN_EWB_TRANS_DETAILS

For Digital signature we have already used one BADI which is provided by SAP – need to check the feasibility of Automation as currently business doing the same process after invoice generation using app VF02

 

Custom CDS to store the Trip Details

Billing Document

Reference Number

After analysis I thought if we can set the background job to VL10A and Create Billing document to create Automatic Delivery PGI and Billing Document (If possible, if yes how to do this)

Looking forward to hearing from community team.

Regards,

Ashish

entry.title: Re: Dynamic authorisation with user attributes in SAP CAP and AMS
entry.content:
Thanks for this article describing setting up AMS with CAP.

But, AMS is neither required for, nor is enabling user attribute-based authorization. Attribute based authorization using was available in CAP from the time @restrict annotation was introduced with almost no complexities. The sample provided can be realized without all the complexities shown, using the "traditional" method.  So, the introduction appears to setup a strawman argument for AMS wrt "traditional" method ignoring that this could be done with "traditional" method as well. Could you please provide an example where AMS is better than the "traditional" method (not using AMS).  I ask because this post appears to be evangelizing use of AMS with CAP.  The argument presented is not convincing to me.

entry.title: 移动设备上启用从“我的收件箱”中的的通知电子邮件直接访问审批详细信息
entry.content:
利益相关

使用 SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 的客户

业务背景

目前，当用户（高级经理，包括采购经理、财务经理、CFO、CEO 等）使用移动设备上的我的收件箱审批付款请求时，他们无法直接从通知电子邮件中的链接直接访问审批的详细信息。相反，用户需要执行以下操作：

  1. 打开通知电子邮件
  2. 单击重定向到“我的收件箱”的链接
  3. 手动找到并点击“打开任务”按钮
  4. 再次单击 进入审批详细信息屏幕

 

 

此附加步骤可显著降低移动设备上的可用性，因为：

  • “打开任务”按钮很难在较小的移动端（直板手机等）屏幕上定位
  • 由于浏览器限制或权限问题，访问可能受到限制，导致审批人无法进入

当前行为

  • 审批通知电子邮件仅将用户重定向到 任务清单视图
  • 用户必须手动单击“打开任务”以访问审批详细信息

因此，由于系统设计的问题，这些中高级管理者通常不会在云ERP里进行日常的操作，因此对系统的操作逻辑不会很熟悉，这会导致他们无法顺畅的在移动设备上完成审批，而是进行繁琐的下一步操作才能进入审批的细节，从而导致延迟和效率低下，从而产生对云ERP系统负面的印象。

预期/所需行为

用户单击审批通知链接时（尤其是通过移动设备）：

  • 系统应直接打开审批详细信息屏幕
  • 在可能的情况下，应绕过 打开任务 按钮
  • 导航应为上下文‑感知（移动设备与台式机）
  • 审批操作应直观、快速且可靠

此行为符合现代移动 UX 标准和云优先原则。

业务影响

当前限制对运营和业务具有重大影响，包括：

  • 延迟审批付款请求
  • 减少移动审批的使用
  • 增加了对桌面访问的依赖
  • 用户对 SAP Fiori UX 的满意度降低
  • 差旅或远程工作情况期间的审批未交付订单

对于财务和管理用户而言，审批通常是供应商付款的关键时刻。如果无法在移动设备上高效审批会直接影响现金流、供应商付款和运营连续性。

受影响的用户组

  • 采购经理及财务经理
  • 高管和高级管理人员
  • 移动审批用户

给客户带来的好处

此增强将会：

  • 提高“我的收件箱”的移动可用性
  • 提高移动审批的采用率
  • 协调 SAP S/4HANA Cloud 与云用户体验期望
  • 减少与审批相关的客户支持案例
  • 巩固 SAP 在高管移动体验方面的定位

请求的功能不会更改业务逻辑，仅更改导航行为，使其成为低风险、高价值 用户体验增强。

请提交您的投票到Enable Direct Access to Approval Details from Notification Email on Mobile Devices in My Inbox

附加注释

此请求与 SAP 的战略方向一致：

  • 移动优先设计
  • SAP Fiori UX 改进
  • 基于云的简单性和自动化

结论

移除对移动用户的“打开任务”按钮的依赖性将显著增强用户体验、提高审批效率并提高 SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 中的整体客户满意度。

entry.title: Re: SAP Joule Studio MCP Setup Guide
entry.content:
@aelghannam  thanks for the feedback.

In this case, I had not used a GitHub repository, since the MCP server was built and deployed directly from the local app structure containing server.js, manifest.yml, and the supporting files. The deployment and debugging path was therefore centered around the Cloud Foundry steps already included in the post, especially cf push and cf logs --recent.

The intent of the blog was to capture the custom MCP server implementation journey itself, including the plain-schema and Zod-based build paths, rather than a GitHub-based reusable package.

On the last issue, the blocking point was not due to an unresolved server-side implementation gap. The MCP server had already progressed through discovery up to tools/list , the remaining issue was a product-side restriction on the tool invocation path, which required SAP product-team involvement for the product-side update rather than a further code fix within the custom MCP server itself.

Hope this helps. 

Regards,

Neetika Tripathi

entry.title: S/4HANA Public Cloud J62: Asset Quantity Resulting from Direct Capitalization with Multiple Componen
entry.content:
Hello everyone,

while working with Scope Item J62 in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud, we observed the following behavior during direct capitalization from procurement.

If an asset is created from multiple PR or PO items that logically form one physical asset, the resulting asset master shows the quantity as the sum of the component quantities. From a business perspective, however, there is only one asset. In this example it’s n standing desk, which has several components.

 

 

 

As far as we know, this is standard SAP behavior in the Public Cloud. However, this can be confusing for asset inventory, reporting, and overall asset transparency.

I would be interested to hear your experiences: Have you encountered the same scenario? Are there recommended best practices or workarounds?

We have also raised this topic via a Customer Influence request to suggest a more flexible solution. Improvement Request Details - Customer Influence


Diana

entry.title: 1H 2026 Latest People Profile: Is manual mapping required for Category Access Control in Production
entry.content:
Hi SAP SuccessFactors Community,

With the universal push to the Latest People Profile coming in the 1H 2026 release, we are currently planning our migration steps for our Production environment.

Based on the SAP Help Guide and KBA 3671975, I understand that the legacy "Employee Views" permissions are no longer supported for the Full Profile. The documented procedure states that we need to:

  1. Download the Employee View's Permission Report (Excel) from the legacy Configure People Profile tool.

  2. Navigate to Configure Latest People Profile > Category Access Control.

  3. Use the Excel export as a reference to manually define the target roles for each new Full Profile category.

Q1-  My doubt/question is regarding the manual effort required for this step. Do we need to do the above steps now itself in Production? But 1H/2026 updates gives a different answer- Role-Based Permissions Available for Full Profile Categories | SAP Help Portal

Q2- Also after 15th May when Latest People Profile updates come to Production do we need again to change anything? Can i go ahead and maintain the translation for the portlets as of now?

 

Regards

Karthik

entry.title: Architecture Decisions for International Trade
entry.content:
Summary

This article provides guidance on defining a target application architecture for International Trade Management capabilities in an SAP-centric landscape.

It is intended as a decision accelerator for enterprise and solution architects, global trade and customs teams, and compliance leaders who need to balance regulatory compliance, operational efficiency, and total cost of ownership when choosing between SAP Global Trade Services (SAP GTS), International Trade capabilities in SAP S/4HANA Cloud, SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Private Edition, and SAP Watch List Screening.

Business Requirements

An overview of the business capabilities include:


Level 3 Business Capabilities in International Trade Management include:

1. Trade Product Classification Management:

  • Ability to manage the organization of goods into a nomenclature according to customs and foreign trade law. i.e., these can be customs tariff numbers or Export Control Classification numbers.

2. Sanctioned Party Screening

  • Ability to manage trade compliance with the sanctioned party list screening across finance, procurement, sales and distribution. If a transaction fails a check, the software blocks it for immediate review.

3. Trade Compliance Management

  • Ability to manage trade compliance with managing licenses required by various legal regulations and embargoed countries to prevent trade transactions to assure compliance business processes.

4. Import Management

  • Ability to manage import processes for goods crossing borders in your supply chain. This includes duty calculations, managing import declarations, and transit movements under customs control. It also includes special customs procedures to leverage financial benefits and enable cross-border manufacturing processes.

5. Export Management

  • Ability to manage export processes for goods crossing borders in your supply chain. This includes managing export declarations and preparation of documentations.

6. Trade Preference Management

  • Ability to manage legal requirements for preferential origin and documentation with suppliers' declaration for your products and processes. Allows to leverage benefits from free trade agreements.
  • Supports multiple calculation methods for preferences based on transaction values, tariff shifts, and via RVC.

7. Intrastat Declaration Management

  • Ability to collect relevant operational and financial data for Intrastat reporting and manage Intrastat declarations to support external reporting requirements to regulatory parties and systems in the European Union. 

 

Solution Options

For International Trade Management, the solution options in the SAP portfolio are:

1. SAP Global Trade Services, edition for SAP HANA

2. SAP S/4HANA Cloud (International Trade Management capabilities)

3. SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Private Edition (International Trade Management capabilities)

4. SAP Watch List Screening

These solutions are often combined into a target architecture pattern rather than used in isolation.

 

Characterization of the Solution Options

Contrary to other business domains, Trade Management is not covered by a single solution; therefore, choosing between several options is not required. Often, customers will combine several of these products into a target architecture pattern rather than choosing only one.

SAP Global Trade Services, edition for SAP HANA

Deployment options for SAP GTS:

For more details about deployment options and transition scenarios, please visit Your way to SAP GTS, edition for SAP HANA (on Premise or Cloud)

Role in the architecture
Dedicated platform for advanced international trade management:

  • Sanctioned party screening, legal control, and embargo checks
  • Import and export management, including electronic customs declarations
  • Management of special customs procedures (bonded warehouses, free trade zones, inward processing, etc.)
  • Trade preference and origin management to leverage free trade agreements

Typical use cases

  • Multinational organizations with high customs and trade complexity
  • Landscapes with multiple ERPs where trade processes must be centralized (international trade hub).
  • Companies with significant duty optimization potential and complex trade compliance risk

 SAP S/4HANA Cloud (International Trade Management), & SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Private.

Role in the architecture

  • Provides embedded capabilities for trade product classification, Intrastat, and selected trade compliance checks
  • Supports key processes such as import/export basics, Intrastat declaration management.
  • Serves as the natural choice in standardized SAP S/4HANA Cloud deployments where trade complexity is low or moderate.
  • For SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Private Edition - typically a natural choice for customers moving from SAP ECC through Brownfield path, using classic Foreign Trade.

Typical use cases

  • Organizations with limited special customs procedures and simpler trade flows.
  • Looking to keep trade capabilities close to core logistics and finance.
  • Scenarios where full SAP GTS is not required, but classification and Intrastat are essential.
  • Used in combination with SAP GTS to cover complex trade compliance requirements.

For more details visit SAP Help – International Trade.


SAP Watch List Screening

Role in the architecture

  • Cloud service for sanctioned party list screening, integrated into finance, procurement, and sales processes.
  • Can be used standalone, with SAP S/4HANA, or complementary to SAP GTS
  • Focused on the Sanctioned Party Screening requirement within the broader International Trade Management capability model.

Typical use cases

  • Organizations starting their trade compliance journey focus on simple screening.
  • Customers who do not require full GTS but must address watch-list obligations quickly.
  • Supplemental screening component in a broader trade compliance architecture.

For more details, please visit SAP Watch List Screening – SAP Help.

 

Criteria to Determine the Most Suitable Target Solution

Regulatory complexity, functional fit, and integration requirements with the existing landscape are the most common criteria used to decide which combination of solutions is appropriate.

1. Regulatory Complexity & Scope

  • Complexity of trade operations (import/export volumes, number of flows and routes)
  • Duty and excise requirements, including special customs procedures (bonded warehouses, free trade zones, inward processing, etc.)
  • Number of countries and jurisdictions in scope today and in the next 3–5 years
  • Overall risk profile and potential penalty exposure for non-compliance

2. Integration Requirements & Application Landscape

  • Single vs. multiple SAP ERP instances (SAP ECC and/or SAP S/4HANA Cloud / PCE)
  •  Presence and criticality of non-SAP ERPs.
  • Need for a central hub (SAP GTS, SAP DRC, SAP PaPM) vs. enabling trade capabilities directly in each ERP system

3. Deployment Options

  • Standalone (compliance hub), or co-deployed with a central ERP instance.

4. Functional Fit

  • Compliance Management
  • Intrastat requirements
  • Customs Management, Foreign Trade Zone & Trade Preferences

Evaluation Spreadsheet

The evaluation spreadsheet provides a structured way to compare the solution options against the criteria above.

Decision Tree


The decision tree is intended to guide architects and business stakeholders through a small number of key questions and arrive at a short list of suitable solution patterns.

 

Typical decision points include:

  • Trade complexity and coverage
  • ERP landscape
  • Immediate compliance needs
 

 

 

Conclusion

International Trade Management is a cross-functional domain that connects logistics, finance and legal requirements. SAP’s portfolio – SAP Global Trade Services, International Trade capabilities in SAP S/4HANA Cloud / SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Private Edition, and SAP Watch List Screening – provides complementary building blocks that can be combined into a coherent architecture rather than chosen in isolation.

For high-complexity international trade and compliance landscapes, SAP Global Trade Services (SAP GTS) is the go-to solution. It provides the breadth and depth needed for complex customs, trade preferences, special procedures, and multi-country risk management, especially when you have multiple ERPs and a high volume of cross-border flows.

The International Trade capabilities in SAP S/4HANA Cloud / SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Private Edition, play a complementary role. They are well-suited for organizations with moderate complexity, or for entities and flows where basic product classification, Intrastat, and standard trade checks are sufficient. They can also be used alongside SAP GTS in a hybrid architecture (for example, embedded trade for simple flows, SAP GTS for high-risk or specialised ones).

SAP Watch List Screening adds another complementary building block. It can be used as a quick way to address sanctioned party screening requirements on top of SAP S/4HANA, or as an additional layer of screening in a SAP GTS-centric landscape.

entry.title: Re: Enhancing Credit Management with Additional Data from Credit Agencies
entry.content:
Hi Rachid,

I'm glad the BAdI can help you in your business scenario.

Regarding your question: yes, the BAdI gives you the freedom to implement this kind of logic.

  • In the documentation BAdI: Derive Values for Formula Parameters in Credit Management, you see a sample code for a rating procedure:
    bpratingprocedure = '2DNB_CF'
  • You can implement the same logic for a different rating procedure to get the information from another credit agency.
  • In your BAdI implementation, you can then compare the two values and select the desired rating to be passed on to SAP Credit Management.
  • As a prerequisite for this to work, credit reports from the respective credit agencies have to be available for the business partner.

Best wishes
Christina

entry.title: CPI iFlow Resource Scanner: How I Built a Simple Web Tool Inside CPI to Find Unused Files
entry.content:
As we work on SAP CPI iFlows over time, we collect extra files. A Groovy script gets added for a new feature, then the feature gets descoped. A Message Mapping gets replaced but the old file stays behind. Soon the resources folder is full of XSD schemas and scripts just sitting there doing nothing.

The problem is that CPI does not show us which files are actually being used. The only option is to click through every step on the canvas to check. This takes too much time, and it needs repeating after every change. Worse, checking the canvas manually misses hidden files - XSD schemas and Groovy UDFs used inside Message Mappings are completely invisible from the canvas view.

This post walks through how I build a resource scanner utility that solves this problem. The tool uses Integration Suite APIs to check the canvas, look inside Message Mappings for hidden dependencies, and report exactly which files are unused. It runs entirely inside CPI and shows results in a browser. The post includes all Groovy scripts, architecture diagrams, and lessons learned from edge cases encountered during development.

1.  Architecture - How It Is Built

The tool is split into two iFlows:

  • UI iFlow: Provides the web page. A GET request returns the HTML interface. A POST request (when you click Scan) forwards to the analysis engine via ProcessDirect.

  • Analysis Engine: Does the actual work of checking files. It only accepts ProcessDirect connections, which means it can be reused by other tools later. This separation has already proven useful.

UI iFlow: GET and POST Request Handling

Analysis Engine: Main Integration Process

2.  The Four Steps of Analysis

Analysis Engine: Local Process Detail

2a.  Step 1: Get the Full List of Files

An OData API call retrieves every file in the iFlow's resource folder:

Endpoint:  https://{baseUrl}/api/v1/IntegrationDesigntimeArtifacts
           (Id='{iFlowID}',Version='active')/Resources

The response gets transformed via XSLT to flatten the Atom XML, then a Groovy script sorts files into four lists:

def xml = new XmlSlurper().parseText(message.getBody(String.class))

def groovyCatalog = [], mmapCatalog = [], xsltCatalog = [], xsdCatalog = []

xml.ResourceList.each { item ->
    def name = item.ResourceName?.text()?.trim()
    def type = item.ResourceType?.text()?.trim()
    
    switch (type) {
        case 'groovy': groovyCatalog << name; break
        case 'mmap':   mmapCatalog << name; break
        case 'xslt':   xsltCatalog << name; break
        case 'xsd':    xsdCatalog << name; break
    }
}

message.setProperty("catalog_groovy", groovyCatalog.join(","))
message.setProperty("catalog_mmap",   mmapCatalog.join(","))
message.setProperty("catalog_xslt",   xsltCatalog.join(","))
message.setProperty("catalog_xsd",    xsdCatalog.join(","))

The lists are stored as exchange properties because each subsequent API call overwrites the message body.

2b.  Step 2: Check the Main Canvas

The tool downloads the iFlow ZIP and extracts the .iflw file - the XML representation of the canvas. It searches for files directly attached to steps.

One thing worth noting: the .iflw uses namespaces that vary between CPI versions. Hardcoding prefixes breaks when moving between environments. Using wildcard traversal avoids this:

def xml = new XmlSlurper().parseText(message.getBody(String.class))

def refGroovy = [] as Set, refMmap = [] as Set
def refXslt = [] as Set, refXsd = [] as Set

xml.'**'.findAll { it.name() == 'property' }.each { prop ->
    def key = prop.@key?.toString()
    def val = prop.@value?.toString()

    if (key == 'script' && val.endsWith('.groovy')) 
        refGroovy << val
    
    else if (key == 'mappinguri') {
        def file = val.tokenize('/').last()
        if (file.endsWith('.mmap')) refMmap << file
        else if (file.endsWith('.xsl') || file.endsWith('.xslt')) refXslt << file
    }
    
    else if (key == 'xsd') {
        def file = val.tokenize('/').last()
        if (file.endsWith('.xsd')) refXsd << file
    }
}

message.setProperty("direct_groovy", refGroovy.join(","))
message.setProperty("direct_mmap",   refMmap.join(","))
message.setProperty("direct_xslt",   refXslt.join(","))
message.setProperty("direct_xsd",    refXsd.join(","))

This finds Groovy scripts on Script steps (key='script'), mappings on Mapping steps (key='mappinguri'), and XSDs on XML Validator steps (key='xsd').

2c.  Step 3: Look Inside Message Mappings

This is the step that provides value beyond what manual inspection can do.

Message Mappings hide files inside them. They reference XSD schemas as source and target structures, and Groovy scripts as UDF libraries. None of this appears on the canvas. A schema can look completely unused while being loaded every time a mapping runs.

The tool opens each .mmap file and extracts these hidden references:

  • lnkRole[@role='SOURCE_IFR_MESS'] → source XSD
  • lnkRole[@role='TARGET_IFR_MESS'] → target XSD
  • lnkRole[@role='UsedFuncLib'] → Groovy UDF

A Splitter processes each mapping file. After the Gather step, a script merges the results:

def mappingList = message.getProperty("direct_mmap")?.split(",")?.findAll { it } ?: []
def indirectXsd = [] as Set, indirectGroovy = [] as Set
def deps = [:]

mappingList.each { name ->
    def xsd = message.getProperty("xsd_refs_for_${name}")?.split(",")?.findAll { it } ?: []
    def gvy = message.getProperty("groovy_refs_for_${name}")?.split(",")?.findAll { it } ?: []
    
    indirectXsd.addAll(xsd)
    indirectGroovy.addAll(gvy)
    deps[name] = [xsd: xsd, groovy: gvy]
}

message.setProperty("indirect_xsd", indirectXsd.join(","))
message.setProperty("indirect_groovy", indirectGroovy.join(","))
message.setProperty("mmap_dependencies", new JsonBuilder(deps).toString())


2d.  Step 4: Do the Math

Resource Comparator: Set Arithmetic and Final JSON Assembly

The tool compares the lists. It takes the full catalog from Step 1 and subtracts everything found in Steps 2 and 3:

Unused Groovy = All_Groovy − (Direct_Groovy ∪ Indirect_Groovy_from_Mappings)
Unused MMAP   = All_MMAP − Direct_MMAP
Unused XSLT   = All_XSLT − Direct_XSLT
Unused XSD    = All_XSD − (Direct_XSD_from_Validator ∪ Indirect_XSD_from_Mappings)
def toSet = { prop -> 
    message.getProperty(prop)?.split(",")?.findAll { it }?.toSet() ?: [] as Set 
}

def catG = toSet("catalog_groovy"), catM = toSet("catalog_mmap")
def catX = toSet("catalog_xslt"),   catS = toSet("catalog_xsd")

def dirG = toSet("direct_groovy"),  dirM = toSet("direct_mmap")
def dirX = toSet("direct_xslt"),    dirS = toSet("direct_xsd")
def indS = toSet("indirect_xsd"),   indG = toSet("indirect_groovy")

def usedG = dirG + indG
def usedS = dirS + indS

def output = [
    summary: [
        total:  [groovy: catG.size(), mmap: catM.size(), xslt: catX.size(), xsd: catS.size()],
        used:   [groovy: usedG.size(), mmap: dirM.size(), xslt: dirX.size(), xsd: usedS.size()],
        unused: [groovy: (catG - usedG).size(), mmap: (catM - dirM).size(), 
                 xslt: (catX - dirX).size(), xsd: (catS - usedS).size()]
    ],
    used: [
        direct: [groovy: dirG as List, mmap: dirM as List, xslt: dirX as List, xsd: dirS as List],
        mmap: new JsonSlurper().parseText(message.getProperty("mmap_dependencies") ?: "{}")
    ],
    unused: [groovy: (catG - usedG) as List, mmap: (catM - dirM) as List,
             xslt: (catX - dirX) as List, xsd: (catS - usedS) as List]
]

message.setBody(new JsonBuilder(output).toPrettyString())
message.setHeader("Content-Type", "application/json")

Whatever is left over is an unused file.

3.  Serving HTML from Groovy

If you are building CPI tools, this technique is worth knowing. You do not need an external server to host a web interface.

CPI's HTTPS adapter returns whatever you put in the message body. Set Content-Type: text/html, return an HTML string from Groovy, and the browser renders it as a normal web page:

Message processData(Message message) {
    def httpMethod = message.getHeaders().get("CamelHttpMethod")

    if (httpMethod == "GET") {
        message.setBody(buildPage())
        message.setHeader("Content-Type", "text/html; charset=UTF-8")
        return message
    }
    return message
}

The HTML page includes JavaScript that posts back to the same URL, receives the JSON from the analysis engine, and renders the dashboard. I have reused this pattern for other utilities.

Here is a sample groovy script to serve the WebUI

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message

def Message processData(Message message) {
    
    def httpMethod = message.getHeaders().get("CamelHttpMethod")
    
    if (httpMethod == "GET") {
        message.setBody(buildPage())
        message.setHeader("Content-Type", "text/html; charset=UTF-8")
    }
    // POST path: JSON flows through from ProcessDirect unchanged
    
    return message
}

def String buildPage() {
    return '''<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<head>
<meta charset="UTF-8">
<title>CPI iFlow Resource Scanner</title>
<style>
:root {
  --navy: #0a1628;
  --blue: #0070f2;
  --teal: #0cc4ac;
  --amber: #f59e0b;
  --gray-100: #f2f4f8;
  --gray-600: #68778f;
  --white: #ffffff;
}
* { box-sizing: border-box; margin: 0; padding: 0; }
body { font-family: system-ui, sans-serif; background: var(--gray-100); }
header { background: linear-gradient(135deg, var(--navy), #1a365d); color: var(--white);
         padding: 16px 24px; display: flex; justify-content: space-between; }
.logo { font-weight: 700; font-size: 16px; }
main { max-width: 1200px; margin: 0 auto; padding: 24px; }
.input-bar { background: var(--white); border-radius: 10px; padding: 16px;
             display: flex; gap: 16px; align-items: flex-end; margin-bottom: 24px; }
.field { display: flex; flex-direction: column; gap: 6px; flex: 1; }
.field label { font-size: 11px; font-weight: 600; text-transform: uppercase; color: var(--gray-600); }
.field input, .field select { padding: 10px; border: 1px solid #ddd; border-radius: 6px; }
.btn { background: var(--blue); color: var(--white); border: none; padding: 10px 24px;
       border-radius: 6px; font-weight: 600; cursor: pointer; }
.btn:disabled { opacity: 0.6; }
.summary-grid { display: grid; grid-template-columns: repeat(4, 1fr); gap: 16px; margin-bottom: 24px; }
.metric-card { background: var(--white); border-radius: 10px; padding: 16px;
               border-top: 3px solid var(--blue); }
.metric-card.unused { border-top-color: var(--amber); }
.metric-title { font-size: 12px; color: var(--gray-600); margin-bottom: 12px; }
.metric-value { font-size: 28px; font-weight: 700; }
.metric-label { font-size: 11px; color: var(--gray-600); }
.panels { display: grid; grid-template-columns: repeat(3, 1fr); gap: 16px; }
.panel { background: var(--white); border-radius: 10px; overflow: hidden; }
.panel-header { padding: 12px 16px; font-weight: 600; border-bottom: 1px solid #eee; }
.panel-body { padding: 12px; }
.resource-item { padding: 6px 8px; font-family: monospace; font-size: 12px; color: #555; }
.resource-item.unused { color: var(--amber); }
#resultsContainer { display: none; }
#resultsContainer.active { display: block; }
</style>
</head>
<body>
<header>
  <div class="logo">CPI iFlow Resource Scanner</div>
  <div style="font-size:12px;opacity:0.7">v1.0</div>
</header>
<main>
  <div class="input-bar">
    <div class="field">
      <label>Tenant</label>
      <select id="tenantSelect"><option>PROD-US</option><option>DEV-US</option></select>
    </div>
    <div class="field" style="flex:2">
      <label>iFlow ID</label>
      <input type="text" id="iflowInput" placeholder="Enter iFlow artifact ID" />
    </div>
    <button class="btn" id="scanBtn" onclick="runScan()">Scan Resources</button>
  </div>
  <div id="resultsContainer"></div>
</main>
<script>
async function runScan() {
  var btn = document.getElementById('scanBtn');
  var container = document.getElementById('resultsContainer');
  btn.disabled = true;
  btn.textContent = 'Scanning...';
  
  try {
    var response = await fetch(window.location.href, {
      method: 'POST',
      headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' },
      body: JSON.stringify({
        tenant: document.getElementById('tenantSelect').value,
        iFlowID: document.getElementById('iflowInput').value
      })
    });
    var data = await response.json();
    container.innerHTML = renderResults(data);
    container.classList.add('active');
  } catch (err) {
    container.innerHTML = '<div style="color:red;padding:20px">Error: ' + err.message + '</div>';
    container.classList.add('active');
  }
  btn.disabled = false;
  btn.textContent = 'Scan Resources';
}

function renderResults(data) {
  var s = data.summary;
  return '<div class="summary-grid">' +
    renderCard('Groovy Scripts', s.total.groovy, s.used.groovy, s.unused.groovy) +
    renderCard('Message Mappings', s.total.mmap, s.used.mmap, s.unused.mmap) +
    renderCard('XSLT Mappings', s.total.xslt, s.used.xslt, s.unused.xslt) +
    renderCard('XSD Schemas', s.total.xsd, s.used.xsd, s.unused.xsd) +
  '</div>' +
  '<div class="panels">' +
    renderPanel('All Resources', data.all, '') +
    renderPanel('Used Resources', data.used.direct, '') +
    renderPanel('Unused Resources', data.unused, 'unused') +
  '</div>';
}

function renderCard(title, total, used, unused) {
  var cls = unused > 0 ? 'metric-card unused' : 'metric-card';
  return '<div class="' + cls + '">' +
    '<div class="metric-title">' + title + '</div>' +
    '<div style="display:flex;gap:20px">' +
      '<div><div class="metric-value">' + total + '</div><div class="metric-label">Total</div></div>' +
      '<div><div class="metric-value">' + used + '</div><div class="metric-label">Used</div></div>' +
      '<div><div class="metric-value">' + unused + '</div><div class="metric-label">Unused</div></div>' +
    '</div></div>';
}

function renderPanel(title, resources, itemClass) {
  var html = '<div class="panel"><div class="panel-header">' + title + '</div><div class="panel-body">';
  ['groovy', 'mmap', 'xslt', 'xsd'].forEach(function(type) {
    var list = resources[type] || [];
    list.forEach(function(name) {
      html += '<div class="resource-item ' + itemClass + '">' + name + '</div>';
    });
  });
  return html + '</div></div>';
}
</script>
</body>
</html>'''
}

4.  What the Dashboard Shows

Full WebUI showing summary metric cards and three-panel result layout

The interface shows:

  • Summary cards with Total / Used / Unused counts per file type
  • Three panels: All Resources, Used Resources, Unused Resources
  • Which XSD schemas and Groovy UDFs are hidden inside each Message Mapping
  • Excel export for documentation

Lessons Learned

While building this tool I encountered a few edge cases which are worth knowing about:

Global Script Bundles. An iFlow can reference Groovy scripts from external Script Collections via the scriptBundleID property. These groovy scripts are not part of the iFlow's own resources folder - they live in a separate, shared package. The scanner will show them as "used" (because they appear in the canvas reference), but they will not appear in the "all" list (because they are not in this iFlow's resources). This is expected behavior, not a bug. If you see a script in "used" that is missing from "all", check whether it comes from a Script Collection.

def isGlobalTarget = parent.children().findAll { it.name() == 'property' }.any { siblingProp ->
    def siblingKey = siblingProp.'**'.find { it.name() == 'key' }?.text()?.trim()
    def siblingValue = siblingProp.'**'.find { it.name() == 'value' }?.text()?.trim()
    
    // Check if the key matches and the value is not null or empty string
    return siblingKey == 'scriptBundleId' && siblingValue
}

Phantom References. Sometimes a Message Mapping internally references a Groovy UDF or XSD that no longer exists in the resources folder. The file may have been deleted, or the mapping was copied from another iFlow without its dependencies. In these cases, Total will not equal Used + Unused - there is a reference to something that is not there. The scanner exposes this inconsistency, which is actually useful: it flags mappings that may fail at runtime.

Commas in Filenames. The scanner stores resource lists as comma-separated strings in exchange properties. If a filename contains a comma (e.g., "Script,Helper,Utils.groovy"), the split logic will treat it as three separate files. This is a delimiter collision. The fix is to avoid commas in filenames - this is good practice anyway since commas are a common delimiter across many tools and systems.

The scanner finds static references - files assigned to steps or declared inside mappings. If a Groovy script constructs a filename at runtime and loads it dynamically, the scanner will not catch that. This is uncommon but worth checking before deleting anything flagged as unused.

Key Takeaways

After reading this post, you should be able to:

  • Serve a browser UI directly from CPI: No external hosting needed. Set Content-Type: text/html and return HTML content from Groovy.
  • Access iFlow internals via OData APIs: The /Resources endpoint gives the full catalog; the ZIP download gives the .iflw canvas file and .mmap mapping files.
  • Detect hidden dependencies: Message Mappings reference XSD schemas and Groovy UDFs internally via lnkRole elements. These are invisible on the canvas but can be extracted by parsing the .mmap XML.
  • Interpret count mismatches: When Total ≠ Used + Unused, it means either an external Script Bundle is in use, or a mapping references a file that no longer exists.
  • Apply this pattern to other tools: The HTML-from-Groovy pattern and the ProcessDirect-based engine separation work for any CPI utility that needs a browser interface.

The JSON output gives a clean list of only the active files. This is useful beyond just cleanup. For example, sending iFlow code to an LLM for documentation works better when you send only the files that are actually in use instead of the entire resource folder. That approach will be covered in the next post.

How do you keep your iFlows clean? Questions, other approaches or edge cases welcome in the comments.

AI Disclosure: AI was used to help structure and refine the language in this post. The technical content, code, and lessons learned are from my own development work.

entry.content: Hi @Noof1   when the (Director) clicks 'transfer System Owner role'  they will be downgraded to Admin role and remain in the system.  i'd recommend that the new person login to the platform first, before you do the role transfer. it shouldn't matter, outcome will be the same, but might help smooth the transition. regards, H

entry.title: Re: RPA Recorder doesn’t detect native file upload popup...
entry.content:
Hi,
you have to record the input file window separately. You should also restart the Desktop Agent before every run (sorry about that, but it works for me this way). Use as few recognition criteria as possible, but it is necessary to use deepness. You have to insert the full file path into the window (do not click to search for the file). Use waits, and use set focus and keystrokes.
Regards, Martina Kolafova

entry.title: Meaning and Usage of Accrual Value Date
entry.content:
The Accrual Value Date is a specific field in a journal entry line item. Technically, this field is known as ACRVALDAT in the table ACDOCA. This field is populated exclusively in journal entry line items that are created by the Accrual Engine. It is utilized in all accrual-related postings performed by the Accrual Engine, such as periodic accrual postings, utilization, and/or release postings. Conversely, in all other types of postings, such as those recording actual costs from goods receipts or supplier invoices, this field remains empty and is not used.

The Accrual Engine uses the accrual value date to determine which accrual, utilization, or release amounts have already been posted for a specific period. Essentially, for entries created by the Accrual Engine, the accrual value date indicates the period to which these entries are assigned. By default, the Accrual Engine utilizes the posting date as the accrual value date.

Exceptions:

  • In certain posting screens of the Accrual Engine, it is possible to enter a posting date (or posting period and fiscal year) that differs from the accrual value date.
  • For Late Utilization (UL) and Late Release (RL, FL) postings, the accrual value date is different from the posting date. Such late postings can only occur if an Accrual Closing Frequency is employed (refer to the separate chapter for more details).

In summary: Suppose an invoice is posted in 2019 but utilizes accruals from 2018. When adding the utilization line items to the invoice, the information that these line items utilize accruals from the previous year (2018) must be stored in these line items. This information is stored in the new field called Accrual Value Date, which would be filled with the date 31.12.2018 in this case.

Since the posting date and document date are fields located in the header and are filled by the invoice itself—likely with a date in 2019—these fields cannot be used by the Accrual Engine. Consequently, a new field called Accrual Value Date had to be introduced in the journal entry line item.

entry.title: Re: Changing condition supplements doesn't create a workl...
entry.content: In standard SAP, only gross price changes (or gross + supplement together) trigger ADA worklist entries. Condition supplements alone are not considered relevant fields. To change this, you’d need either customizing or an enhancement.
